title: Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation
learningObjectives: Explain Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation with phase-5 depth and clarity
Implement Java examples for Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation with production awareness
Identify failure risks and mitigation strategies
Reason about trade-offs under realistic constraints
Design or optimize solutions with maintainability in mind
Present interview answers with concrete evidence and structure

sections.title: Why Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation matters
sections.content:
Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation is a high-value Day 47 topic in Phase 5 (Spring ecosystem and enterprise backend patterns).

At this stage, interviewers evaluate whether you can connect implementation details with reliability, scalability, and maintainability outcomes.

What strong answers include:
1. precise concept framing,
2. operational implications,
3. one realistic failure pattern,
4. one practical mitigation.

This topic is not just about code that compiles. It is about decisions that survive production traffic and team ownership.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation
sections.content:
### Day 47: Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation
Phase context: Spring ecosystem and enterprise backend patterns.

### What to understand deeply
- mechanism and boundaries
- runtime behavior and cost
- operational failure modes
- trade-offs vs alternatives

### Implementation workflow
1. Build a clear baseline solution.
2. Validate edge/failure paths.
3. Measure impact (latency, memory, throughput, or complexity).
4. Refactor for readability and maintainability.

### Interview framing template
- "The problem this solves is..."
- "I chose this approach because..."
- "The key trade-off is..."
- "A failure case is... and mitigation is..."

### Production perspective
For Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation, quality is judged by correctness + resilience + debuggability, not just API usage.

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**Spring Boot testing slices:** `@WebMvcTest`, `@DataJpaTest` — fast tests with minimal context.

**`@MockBean`** overrides production beans — document why test differs from prod wiring.

**Testcontainers** for integration tests — real DB/Kafka closer to production than H2 alone.

**Contract tests** at API boundary — consumer-driven contracts catch breaking JSON changes.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: OAuth2 roles: resource owner, client, authorization server, resource server.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: JWT three segments (header.payload.sig) separated by dots — parse parts.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Scope check: token must contain required scope for the operation.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 47
sections.description: Visual model for Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation

rectangle "Satyverse(Satyam Parmar) Lesson Engine" {

Learner --> "Theory" : Understand concept
Learner --> "Code" : Implement
Learner --> "Exercise" : Practice
Learner --> "Interview Drill" : Explain
@enduml

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Relying on Spring defaults without understanding lifecycle and bean scope.
Leaking transaction boundaries across service methods.
Ignoring lazy loading and query shape in persistence logic.
Implementing security rules without end-to-end flow clarity.
Using framework annotations mechanically without design intent.

sections.title: Exercise - Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation Applied Scenario
sections.problem:
Implement a realistic solution for **Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation**.

Requirements:
1. Define assumptions and constraints first.
2. Include one failure-path handling strategy.
3. Provide one improvement over baseline.
4. State complexity or runtime impact.
5. Keep implementation review-friendly.
hints: Add one observability/debug aid in output or comments.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is OAuth2 roles?
conceptual.answer: Resource owner, client, authorization server, resource server—clear separation.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how does **Spring Security - OAuth2 & Authorisation** show up in **production** debugging or **on-call**?
followUps.answer: Tie **logs**, **metrics**, **repro** steps, and **ownership** to what you already said. **Interviewers** reward **symptom → cause → fix → verification**, not textbook **definitions** alone. Resource owner, client, authorization server, resource server—clear separation. Close with **how you would verify** the answer still holds after a change. wrongAnswers: JWTs cannot be revoked before expiry.
Client secret is safe to embed in single-page applications.
OAuth2 is only for user login buttons, not machine auth.
Resource server should trust any JWT signed by anyone if signature verifies.
Authorization code flow works without redirect URIs.
Refresh tokens should never be rotated.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Phase 5 Expectation | Quick Rule | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Framing | Explain intent + constraints | Start from problem | API-only answer |
| Design | State trade-off explicitly | Compare alternatives | One-solution bias |
| Runtime | Mention cost impact | Time + space/ops | No performance reasoning |
| Failure | Name one incident pattern | Mitigation path | Happy-path only thinking |
| Communication | Be concise + concrete | Use one example | Generic buzzwords |
